questions.question:
Let G be an undirected complete graph on n vertices, where n > 2. Then, the number of different Hamiltonian cycles in G is equal to

A. n!/2
B. (n-1)!
C. (n-1)!/2
D. n!/(2n)
questions.to_find: Number of distinct Hamiltonian cycles in K_n

understand.plain: A Hamiltonian cycle visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the start. In K_n, we fix one vertex to avoid counting rotations, arrange the remaining n-1 vertices in (n-1)! ways, then divide by 2 because traversing the cycle in reverse gives the same cycle. The expressions (n-1)!/2 and n!/(2n) are algebraically identical, so both C and D are correct.

keywords.explain: A closed path that visits every vertex of the graph exactly once before returning to the starting vertex.
keywords.example: In K_4 with vertices {1,2,3,4}, the cycle 1-2-3-4-1 is Hamiltonian.

keywords.explain: A graph in which every pair of distinct vertices is connected by a unique edge; it has n*(n-1)/2 edges.
keywords.example: K_5 has 10 edges and every vertex has degree 4.

keywords.explain: A Hamiltonian cycle is the same regardless of which vertex you start from, so we fix one vertex and permute the rest, giving (n-1)! orderings before dividing by 2 for direction.

given.aim: Count the number of distinct Hamiltonian cycles in the complete graph K_n.

terms.meaning: Number of vertices in the complete graph K_n (n > 2)
terms.example: n = 5 gives K_5 with 12 distinct Hamiltonian cycles

terms.meaning: Fix one vertex, permute remaining n-1, divide by 2 for direction symmetry
Total permutations n! divided by n (rotations) and 2 (direction) equals (n-1)!/2
terms.connects: Option D, algebraically same as C

given.plan: Fix vertex 1 to break rotational symmetry, arrange the remaining n-1 vertices in (n-1)! ways, then divide by 2 because each cycle can be traversed in two directions. Verify option D = n!/(2n) = (n-1)!/2, confirming both C and D are correct.

steps.title: Fix one vertex to eliminate rotation duplicates
steps.apply: Fix vertex 1. The remaining n-1 vertices can be arranged in (n-1)! ways, each forming a unique sequence 1-v_2-v_3-...-v_n-1.
steps.note: Without fixing one vertex, each distinct cycle would be counted n times (once per starting vertex).

steps.title: Divide by 2 for direction symmetry
steps.apply: Each cycle 1-v_2-...-v_n-1 is the same as its reverse 1-v_n-...-v_2-1. Divide by 2: answer = (n-1)!/2.

steps.title: Verify option D equals option C
steps.apply: n!/(2n) = n*(n-1)!/(2n) = (n-1)!/2. Option D equals option C.
steps.note: Both C and D express the same quantity; this is an MSQ with both correct.

solution.result: C, D -- both (n-1)!/2 and n!/(2n) equal the number of distinct Hamiltonian cycles in K_n.

questions.formula_note: The key formula is Hamiltonian cycles in K_n = (n-1)!/2; since n!/(2n) simplifies to the same expression, both options C and D are correct, making this an MSQ.

questions.question:
Let G be any connected, weighted, undirected graph.

I. G has a unique minimum spanning tree, if no two edges of G have the same weight.
II. G has a unique minimum spanning tree, if, for every cut of G, there is a unique minimum-weight edge crossing the cut.

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. I only
B. II only
C. Both I and II
D. Neither I nor II
questions.to_find: Which statements about MST uniqueness are true

understand.plain: A minimum spanning tree (MST) is unique when there is no ambiguity in edge selection. Statement I is true because distinct edge weights force a unique greedy choice at every step of Kruskal's algorithm. Statement II is also true and is the general necessary and sufficient condition: an MST is unique if and only if every graph cut has a unique minimum-weight crossing edge.

keywords.explain: A spanning tree of a connected weighted graph with the smallest possible total edge weight.
keywords.example: In a 4-node graph, the MST uses exactly 3 edges with minimum total weight.

keywords.explain: A partition of vertices into two disjoint sets S and V-S; edges crossing the cut connect one vertex in S to one in V-S.
keywords.example: Splitting {1,2} and {3,4} in K_4 gives 4 crossing edges.

keywords.explain: The minimum-weight edge crossing any cut of the graph must be included in every MST.
keywords.example: If edge (u,v) is uniquely lightest across a cut, it appears in all MSTs.

understand.visual_alt: A weighted undirected graph with a highlighted spanning tree showing how the minimum crossing edge for each cut is uniquely chosen.

given.aim: Determine whether the two given conditions each guarantee a unique MST.

terms.example: Any road network with unique road lengths

terms.meaning: All edge weights distinct implies unique MST
terms.example: Kruskal's: no two edges tie, so each greedy choice is forced

terms.meaning: Unique min-weight cut edge for every cut implies unique MST
terms.example: Cut property: the unique lightest crossing edge must be in every MST
terms.connects: Necessary and sufficient condition for uniqueness

given.plan: Verify Statement I using Kruskal's algorithm (distinct weights force unique edge selection). Verify Statement II using the cut property: if every cut's minimum edge is unique, no two MSTs can differ.

steps.title: Verify Statement I: distinct weights imply unique MST
steps.formula_raw: If all edge weights are distinct, then MST is unique.
steps.apply: In Kruskal's algorithm, edges are sorted by weight. Since all weights are distinct, at each step only one edge is the current minimum safe edge to add. No tie-breaking is needed, so the MST is unique.
steps.note: This is a well-known sufficient condition. Statement I is TRUE.

steps.title: Verify Statement II: unique min cut edge implies unique MST
steps.formula_raw: MST is unique iff every cut has a unique minimum-weight crossing edge.
steps.apply: By the cut property, the minimum-weight edge crossing any cut must be in every MST. If each cut's minimum edge is unique, there is no freedom in choosing different edges, so the MST is forced to be unique.
steps.note: This is the exact necessary and sufficient condition. Statement II is TRUE.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Both Statement I and Statement II are true. The answer is C.
steps.note: Statement I is a sufficient condition; Statement II is both necessary and sufficient.

solution.result: C. Both I and II are TRUE.

questions.formula_note: The MST uniqueness criterion is central: distinct edge weights (Statement I) is a sufficient condition, and unique minimum cut-crossing edge for every cut (Statement II) is the necessary and sufficient condition; both guarantee MST uniqueness.

questions.question:
Let G = (V, E) be an undirected unweighted connected graph. The diameter of G is defined as:

diam(G) = max_{u,v} {the length of shortest paths between u and v}

Let M be the adjacency matrix of G. Define graph G_2 on the same set of vertices with adjacency matrix N, where

N_{ij} = 1 if (M_{ij} > 0) or (M^2_{ij} > 0), where P = M^2
N_{ij} = 0 otherwise

Which one of the following statements is true?

A. diam(G_2) <= ceil(diam(G)/2)
B. diam(G_2) >= ceil(diam(G)/2)
C. diam(G_2) = ceil(diam(G)/2)
D. diam(G_2) = diam(G) - 1
questions.to_find: Relationship between diameter of G_2 (graph square) and diameter of G

understand.plain: The graph G_2 (the square of G) connects two vertices if they are within distance 2 of each other in G. Any path of length d in G can be halved in G_2 by taking two-hop shortcuts, giving a path of length ceil(d/2). Therefore diam(G_2) is at most ceil(diam(G)/2). The inequality may be strict (not always equality), making option A correct.

keywords.explain: G_2 has the same vertices as G; two vertices are adjacent in G_2 if their distance in G is at most 2.
keywords.example: In path P_5, vertices at positions 1 and 3 become adjacent in G_2 since dist=2.

keywords.explain: The maximum over all pairs of vertices of the shortest-path distance between them.
keywords.example: A path graph P_5 has diameter 4.

keywords.explain: Entry (M^2)_{ij} counts walks of length 2 from i to j; it is positive iff i and j share a common neighbor.
keywords.example: If M_{ik}=1 and M_{kj}=1, then (M^2)_{ij} >= 1, so N_{ij}=1 in G_2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='18' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>G (path, diam=4) vs G_2 (diam=2)</text><line x1='30' y1='80' x2='100' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='170' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='170' y1='80' x2='240' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='240' y1='80' x2='310' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='30' cy='80' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='100' cy='80' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='170' cy='80' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='240' cy='80' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='310' cy='80' r='10' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>u</text><text x='310' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>v</text><path d='M30,55 Q100,25 170,55' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M170,55 Q240,25 310,55' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>G_2 red arcs halve diam from 4 to 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Path graph P_5 with original edges (grey) and the G_2 two-hop shortcut edges (red arcs) that reduce the diameter from 4 to 2.

given.aim: Find the correct inequality relating diam(G_2) to diam(G) for any connected unweighted graph G.

terms.meaning: Diameter of graph G: maximum shortest-path distance over all vertex pairs
terms.connects: Upper-bounds diam(G_2) via the halving argument

terms.meaning: Graph on same vertices as G; edge (i,j) exists iff dist_G(i,j) <= 2
terms.example: In P_5, vertices 1 and 3 become adjacent in G_2
terms.connects: Defined by N = (M or M^2 positive)

terms.meaning: Upper bound on diam(G_2): each two-hop shortcut halves effective path length
terms.connects: The key bound in option A

given.plan: For any pair u,v at distance d in G, the shortest path can be traversed in G_2 in ceil(d/2) steps by pairing consecutive edges. So diam(G_2) <= ceil(diam(G)/2). The bound need not be tight, so the answer is <= not =.

steps.title: Understand G_2 adjacency from the matrix definition
steps.apply: If M_{ij}>0, vertices i and j are direct neighbors. If (M^2)_{ij}>0, there exists k with path i-k-j of length 2 in G. So N_{ij}=1 iff dist_G(i,j) is 1 or 2.
steps.note: G_2 is the graph square: an edge exists iff the distance in G is at most 2.

steps.title: Prove diam(G_2) <= ceil(diam(G)/2)
steps.apply: Take u, v with dist_G(u,v) = d and path u=w_0,w_1,...,w_d=v. In G_2, pair steps: w_0-w_2, w_2-w_4, ... Each pair is a single G_2 edge. Path length in G_2 is ceil(d/2). So dist_{G_2}(u,v) <= ceil(d/2) for all pairs, hence diam(G_2) <= ceil(diam(G)/2).
steps.note: For example, P_5 has diam(G)=4 and diam(G_2)=2=ceil(4/2). Equality holds here but need not in general.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Option A states diam(G_2) <= ceil(diam(G)/2), which is always true. Option C claims equality (not always true). Options B and D are incorrect.

questions.formula_note: The graph-square-diameter formula establishes diam(G_2) <= ceil(diam(G)/2); squaring the graph at most halves the diameter by enabling two-hop shortcuts, but the bound is not always tight, so <= is correct rather than =.

questions.question: Consider a simple undirected graph of 10 vertices. If the graph is disconnected, then the maximum number of edges it can have is _______________ .
questions.to_find: Maximum number of edges in a disconnected simple graph on 10 vertices

understand.plain: To maximise edges while keeping the graph disconnected, we isolate one vertex and put all remaining edges among the other 9 vertices. A complete graph K_9 on those 9 vertices gives C(9,2) = 36 edges, while the isolated 10th vertex contributes 0 edges. Adding any edge from the isolated vertex would connect the graph, so 36 is the maximum.

keywords.explain: A graph where at least one vertex cannot reach some other vertex; it has more than one connected component.
keywords.example: Vertices {1,...,10} where vertex 10 has no edges is disconnected.

keywords.explain: Number of edges in a complete graph on n vertices.
A vertex with degree 0; including one isolated vertex forces disconnectedness at minimum edge cost.
keywords.example: Vertex 10 with no edges keeps the graph disconnected while vertices 1-9 form K_9.

understand.visual_alt: K_9 complete graph on 9 vertices with all 36 edges, plus one isolated vertex 10 with no connections, forming the maximum-edge disconnected simple graph on 10 vertices.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of edges in a simple disconnected undirected graph on 10 vertices.

terms.meaning: Total number of vertices in the graph
Graph must have at least 2 connected components
terms.example: Minimum cost: isolate exactly one vertex
terms.connects: Forces at most n-1 = 9 vertices in the densest component

terms.meaning: Complete graph on 9 vertices achieves maximum edges for a 9-vertex connected component
terms.connects: The optimal construction: K_(n-1) plus one isolated vertex

given.plan: Isolate one vertex and make all other 9 vertices form K_9. Verify this beats any other disconnected partition, then count C(9,2) = 36.

steps.title: Choose optimal disconnected structure
steps.formula_raw: Max edges in disconnected simple graph on n vertices = C(n-1, 2) = (n-1)*(n-2)/2
steps.apply: Isolate vertex 10 (degree 0). Place remaining 9 vertices in K_9. Any edge from vertex 10 would connect the graph. Alternative splits give fewer edges: e.g., K_8 + K_2 gives C(8,2)+C(2,2) = 28+1 = 29 < 36.
steps.note: The maximum is always achieved by K_(n-1) plus one isolated vertex.

steps.title: Count edges in K_9
steps.apply: K_9 has C(9,2) = 9*8/2 = 36 edges. The isolated vertex contributes 0 edges. Total = 36.

questions.formula_note: The max-edges-disconnected-graph formula C(n-1,2) is key: by isolating one vertex and making the rest a complete graph K_9, we achieve C(9,2) = 36, the maximum possible edges in a disconnected simple graph on 10 vertices.
